# Writing Beats

The user supplies a Markdown pile of raw material. This is **exploit**: exploration is done, so commit to a path through the fixed pile without outlining the whole article.

If no destination path was supplied, ask once and remember it.

1. Establish what the audience already knows—the concepts grounded as prerequisites.
2. Offer 2–3 genuinely different starting beats drawn from the pile. Each may rely only on grounded concepts; say what new concepts it grounds and what paths that unlocks.
3. After the user chooses, write only that beat to the article file.
4. Re-read the article from disk, update the grounded set, and offer 2–3 reachable next beats.
5. Repeat one beat at a time until the journey reaches a natural end.

## Grounding

Every concept must be grounded before a beat can lean on it. A concept is grounded because the audience brings it as a prerequisite or an earlier beat introduced it. Where a concept has a term, land the idea and term together.

Each beat requires already-grounded concepts and may ground new ones. A candidate is reachable only when all of its requirements are grounded. The main lever is what to assume versus what to teach: too many prerequisites exclude readers; too much early grounding drowns the opening.

Pull material from the pile by paraphrasing, splitting, recombining, or quoting. Append one beat at a time, re-read before every write, and preserve user edits. The journey ends when complete, not when the pile is empty.
